versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "I am the vine, you are the branches; he who abides in Me and I in him, he bears much fruit, for apart from Me you can do nothing."

context_lines: In **John 15**, Jesus uses the vine and branches to teach abiding, fruitfulness, obedience, love, and dependence on Him. Requests in this chapter are framed by ongoing union with Christ, not by detached human wishing.
explanation_lines: Today, however, holiness is often misunderstood — either distorted into harsh legalism or dismissed entirely in favor of comfort. In the Catholic tradition, holiness is frequently associated with ascetic practices, monastic ideals, or earning merit through sacraments and penance — making grace feel distant and conditional. Among Protestant traditions, holiness can become synonymous with behavioral conformity, especially in churches that emphasize external rules, dress codes, or denominational distinctives over inner transformation. Meanwhile, many non-denominational churches, in an effort to emphasize God's acceptance and grace, often stop short of calling believers to the deeper work of repentance, self-denial, and obedience. Grace is rightly preached, but without this balance, it becomes a message of comfort rather than transformation.

True holiness is not perfectionism — it is alignment with the heart of God. It is born not from fear of punishment, but from love for the One who has already rescued us. It is Spirit-fueled, not self-willed. To recover holiness in the Church today means to preach grace that not only pardons, but purifies; to teach obedience not as bondage, but as beauty. **The world needs less polished Christians and more surrendered ones — men and women who live set apart not to appear holy, but because they have been wholly remade.**
